g.onclick: sendPrompt('Why is The a single token?')
sendPrompt('Why is cat a single token?')
sendPrompt('Why is sat a single token?')
sendPrompt('Why is on a single token?')
sendPrompt('Why is the a single token?')
sendPrompt('Why is mat a single token?')

svg.text: Step 3 — map each token to an integer ID (vocabulary lookup)

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What is a token ID in an LLM?')
sendPrompt('What is a token ID in an LLM?')
sendPrompt('What is a token ID in an LLM?')
sendPrompt('What is a token ID in an LLM?')
sendPrompt('What is a token ID in an LLM?')
sendPrompt('What is a token ID in an LLM?')

svg.text: Note: "The" and "the" both map to ID 262 — same token
Step 4 — look up each ID in the embedding matrix → a vector

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What is a token embedding vector?')
g.text: e.g. 100,000 tokens × 4,096 dimensions — one row per token

svg.text: Step 5 — each token is now a high-dimensional vector

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How do embedding vectors encode word meaning?')
sendPrompt('How do embedding vectors encode word meaning?')
sendPrompt('How do embedding vectors encode word meaning?')

svg.text: Similar words land close together in vector space — "cat" and "kitten" are neighbours.
The model never sees raw text again after this step — only vectors.
